subject: History
chapter: Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement

questions.q: Who among the following was the main inspiration behind the Non-Cooperation Movement?
Describe the significance of the Salt March in the Indian National Movement.
questions.answer: The Salt March was a significant event in the Indian National Movement as it was a peaceful protest against the British salt tax. It started on March 12, 1930, and ended on April 6, 1930. The march was led by Mahatma Gandhi and his followers from Ahmedabad to Dandi, a village on the coast, to protest against the British salt tax. The march drew international attention and sparked widespread protests across India.

questions.q: Fill in the blank: The _______ Act of 1919 was passed by the British government, which led to widespread protests across India.
What were the main features of the Quit India Movement?
questions.answer: The Quit India Movement was a mass movement launched by Mahatma Gandhi on August 8, 1942. The main features of the movement were: (i) it was a call for immediate independence, (ii) it was a mass movement that involved people from all walks of life, (iii) it was a peaceful movement that used non-violent methods of protest, and (iv) it was a movement that aimed to unite Indians across caste, class, and religious lines.

questions.q: True or False: The Non-Cooperation Movement was a complete success.
What were the consequences of the Jallianwala Bagh massacre?
questions.answer: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre led to widespread outrage and protests across India. It marked a turning point in the Indian National Movement, as it led to a shift from moderate to radical nationalism. The massacre also led to the resignation of several British officials and the eventual passage of the Government of India Act of 1919.

questions.q: Who among the following was a key figure in the Indian National Movement, known for his advocacy of non-violence and civil disobedience?
Describe the role of women in the Indian National Movement.
questions.answer: Women played a significant role in the Indian National Movement. They participated in protests, demonstrations, and civil disobedience movements. Women like Sarojini Naidu, Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay, and Aruna Asaf Ali were key figures in the movement. They worked tirelessly to mobilize support for the movement and to promote the ideals of nationalism and self-reliance.

questions.q: Fill in the blank: The _______ Conference was a significant event in the Indian National Movement, where Mahatma Gandhi and other leaders discussed the future of India.
What were the main differences between the Moderate and Extremist factions of the Indian National Congress?
questions.answer: The main differences between the Moderate and Extremist factions of the Indian National Congress were: (i) approach to British rule - Moderates believed in cooperating with the British, while Extremists believed in confronting them, (ii) methods of protest - Moderates believed in peaceful and constitutional methods, while Extremists believed in more radical and militant methods, and (iii) goals - Moderates wanted gradual reforms, while Extremists wanted complete independence.
